author: @zeronim1
text: It incorporates a vortex for a 200% efficiency increase. A base covered with ash, terakota flowerpot coated with graphite (again thanks 10c increase in temperature). A two layer can (two cans) with a forever wick between them (thanks for that :)). Ethanol  flame source a 40cm glass tube made of heat resistant glass made of borosilicate, with a vortex producing base. And a plower pot plate with an upside down bowl filled with sand on top hanging above the tube's flame. Should the tube be longer or made from metal mosquito netting? The flame might be too hot for that xD

author: @zeronim1

author: @dariusj33
text:
I've got 3 questions:

1. Where is your smoke sensor? How didn't it kick in?
2. Are those ceiling panels flammable?
3. Does your insurance company know what you're doing?

Bonus question?
4. Why does it burn less efficiently (i.e. more flame) when the mantle is on? You said before that no flame == more efficient burning. The vortex should still be sucking in enough oxygen - why doesn't it then?

author: @chronosferatu345
text: Maybe this is a dangerous and/or foolish question, but just how high will a mantle take a flame? It seems like the mantle somehow keeps the flame going ever higher as long as there is mantle around the flame.

author: @Flashahol
text: A version that would be modular enough to take both pellets and oil would be fantastic!

author: @KeithOlson
text: The mantle also has the bonus of working as a spark arrester; any that make it through the mesh will be too small to start a fire.

author: @Spedley_2142
text:
The purpose of a mantle is to get hot and radiate heat (or light). To do this you have to force the exhaust gases through it.
I would try an inverted cone in the top so that the diameter narrows as it rises. In theory the extra pressure at the top should force more gas out at the bottom.
Or perhaps a simpler solution would be a cone shaped mantle, perhaps even doubled up to increase resistance (or a cap on top).

author: @AutoNomades
text:
About the woodn fuel, here in France eveything gets very expensive, and industrialized "liquid" wood (pellets) becomes very expensive (it's a bit a woods monopoly mafia around, since it can be overindustrialized..)
But wood can be harvested for free out of this circuit. I eard of a grandma having regularely pruned willows in her garden, doing it with a pair of shears every winter. It is a light wood, but it grows fast and is easily cutted by a grandma's power ^^ It was her heating source, right in her garden !! For less needing of feeding, there is the gravity fed rocket stove, the batchbox...
 A good combinaison of willow is to use it in a greywater artificial wetland filtration system.

author: @heywoodjabozoff9383
text:
this is the problem i had with the rocket stove : it burns too quickly.
On the other end of the spectrum is the woodgas stove; the problem with that is the risk of the fire going out, so a fan is useful. If there were a way, like the throttle regulator on a gas engine, to increase air depending on the heat output, to control air intake to slow down the burn without extinguishing the flame, that would be the best of both designs.

author: @thelightbearer8965
text: I would love to see you do a home made digester with a gas collector. You can then adapt a petrol generator to start on fuel and then switch over to the gas from the generator. Gas gets cleaned by a home made activated carbon scrubber. You then have a generator making electricity for the house via a bank of batteries. And you also have gas you can cook on via a low pressure  gas cooker like a cadac or camping stove. It could work as a complete off grid sullution. And if you keep animals as a food source, they will also greatly improve your gas yield if you are adding their effluent to the gas digester. Its a very simple thing to make tbh.

author: @xpdnc9794
text:
Rob, I made a couple of these a few decades ago to provide heat and cooking facilities in the outback remote station camps for the Jackaroos here in Australia. From my experiments the vortex doesn't have to be as large as you made it and make sure you have the vortex going in the right direction, clockwise in the southern hemisphere and anti clockwise in the northern. 
I ended up taking the vortex out due to melting pots and pans.
Great videos, keep it up.

FIRE 🔥🔥🔥🔥 !  😱
LOL 😀! 
Needs a radiator and copper coil system so the stove can go outside. It would need a flue or chimney system to be safer to use the stove inside. If it's over 4kw you would need ventilation or an air intake coming from outside depending on the size room you are putting it in. A gravity fed pellet , old timber or pallet wood system would be great. 
Keep em coming.! 🔥👍

author: @corringhamdepot4434
text: Reminded me of being inspired by YouTube videos to try blasting air into the base of my garden incinerator. So I inserted 2 lengths of old stainless steel hoover tube into the bottom of the incinerator. Connected the old hoover hose to the hose of my workshop vac hose with tape. Set it to blow and switched on. Massive flames blew out of all of the holes around the incinerator, and from around the bottom of the lid.  Success as a "proof of concept", but I am now looking for a suitable metal container without so many holes. 🔥🧯🙂

author: @jdhtyler
text:
CORN burner from a hopper outside the farmhouse with a worm feed, My sister in Canada had this setup back in 2018. Easy to grow dry and store compared to wood pellets ?
One interesting point is they harvest the corn around October so that it is as dry as possible; the corn is usually sent for storage and they weigh it and then dry it then weigh it again and charge you for the moisture removed.

author: @darrylpang3903
text:
If you want to control the burn rate, perhaps you could try to throttle the air intake.

The rocket stove you have burns wet. You can tell that it doesn't take in enough air because adding the hairdryer helped it burn harder. I think you could try and experiment with a flap for the air intake that can be held open at various angles. Being able to control the amount of air that enters should help you control how hard it burns, but it might result in a sooty flame as more incomplete combustion takes place.

replies.author: @AlbertBergen
replies.text: Yes, at three different points.  how much air comes in behind the wood, how much air comes in under the wood, and how much air comes in for the secondary burn.  I'm curious how you solve the indoor air quality issue with a heater like that - connect it to a conventional chimney?  Pellet-stove style vent?  Might make something like it for outdoor use, just to watch stuff burn in it.

replies.author: @darrylpang3903
replies.text:
That or put a sleeve on the mantle to reduce the amount of extra oxygen the gas inside gets. That should reduce the burn.

Or how about routing the chimney of the rocket stove through a thermal mass, like a block of sand or water? That will help you to retain the heat generated for longer.

replies.author: @darrylpang3903
replies.text:
@AlbertBergen the problem with my suggestion is that reducing oxygen results in incomplete combustion, which means nasty gases like carbon monoxide are generated.

Perhaps instead of reducing air, the fuel could be reduced. Instead of a hopper as big as that, Rob could use a funnel instead. The funnel would allow Rob to hold wood ready to burn while still limiting the amount of wood exposed to the heat and fire below.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
@darrylpang3903 maybe send fuel down a sloping length of metal U channel?
Could  run all way through with door on bottom for when ash needs removed. A little rake that fits inside the chanel might work well for pulling the Ash out

author: @colfaxschuyler3675
text:
Is there a difference between stainless steel and galvanized steel?
Galvanized is readily available locally.

replies.author: @zenile7087
replies.text:
Stainless steel contains at least 13% of chromium, plus nickel, sometimes molybdenum, depending of the severity of corrosion.

The linear dilatation when heated is terrible, 3 times more than classic steel, covered or not with zinc.

So using stainless steels can provides lots lots of issues.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: Good idea, as long as you burn off the galvanized completely as its highly toxic.  But it will work!

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: Oh, also if it's a long term project, you want to use stainless as it will last a lot longer.

replies.author: @colfaxschuyler3675
replies.text:
@l0I0I0I0 Well, it isn't meant to be a disposable heater.  :-D

I am saddened, as galvanized is cheap as all get out.  Off to Amazon, I guess.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0

author: @zenile7087
text:
Hi from France, you have made a very nice twirl, maybe the Coriolis Effect plays a role here. Have you taken it here ?

Or could you made here an opposite sense of twirling for providing a "visual proof" ? 

Very well done, maybe an annular ash tray could be useful to avoid any zig-zag hot point ^^

Very fine stainless steel can be found also.

Spot resistance welding can also be used with extra-long arms for such narrow and deep pipewire linear weld.

Best regards & greetings !
